title: The part that uses substances to manage what nothing else can
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

 I know this part. I've sat with it, fought it, hated it, and finally learned to listen to it....
metaDescription:
The part that uses substances to manage what nothing else can 

 I know this part. I've sat with it, fought it, hated it, and finally learned to listen to...
htmlContent:
The part that uses substances to manage what nothing else can

I know this part. I've sat with it, fought it, hated it, and finally learned to listen to it. It's the part that reaches for the drink when the internal noise gets too loud. The part that rolls the joint when the memories press too close. The part that pops the pill when the body won't stop vibrating with a terror that has no name. And here's the thing. This part is not your enemy. It's not a weakness. It's not a moral failure. It's a survival strategy that your system built because nothing else worked.

Let me say that again. This part exists because you needed it. Your nervous system, faced with overwhelming pain that no human being should have to carry alone, found a way to make it bearable. Even if just for a moment. Even if the cost came later. That's not a character flaw. That's a creative, desperate, intelligent response to an impossible situation.

I used to believe that if I could just get rid of this part, I'd be free. I'd white-knuckle through sobriety, hate myself for every craving, and shame myself for every slip. Sound familiar? It took me years to understand that the part using substances wasn't the problem. It was the symptom of a deeper wound that nobody had ever helped me tend to.

What this part actually does for you

Think about it. When you're in the middle of a flashback, when your body is reliving something that happened decades ago, when your nervous system is screaming that you're in danger right now even though you're safe, what can actually stop that? Not logic. Not reassurance. Not someone telling you to breathe. Sometimes the only thing that can interrupt that cascade of terror is a chemical intervention. And your part figured that out. It found a way to give you relief when no one else could.

This is where we have to get honest about what the wellness industry gets wrong. They'll sell you meditation apps and breathing exercises and positive affirmations. But if your nervous system is in a full-throttle trauma response, none of that touches it. Right? You can't breathe your way out of a flashback when your brainstem believes you're about to die. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades showing us that trauma lives in the body, not just the mind. And sometimes the body needs something more than a calming voice to come back into the present.

The part that uses substances is doing a job. It's managing what nothing else in your life has been able to manage. The unbearable aloneness. The shame that feels like it's eating you from the inside. The rage that has nowhere to go. The grief that's so big you're afraid it will drown you. This part steps in and says, "I've got this. I'll take the edge off. I'll make it possible for you to get through the night." And it's been doing that for years, maybe decades, without a single thank you.

The problem isn't the part. It's the strategy.

Here's where it gets complicated. The strategy works, until it doesn't. The drink that calms you down one night might send you into a shame spiral the next morning. The drug that helps you sleep might make the nightmares worse when it wears off. The substance that quiets the inner critic might leave you with a hangover that makes the critic scream even louder. Janina Fisher, who wrote extensively about structural dissociation and trauma, would say this part is trying to protect you from the feelings that feel too big to survive. But it's using a blunt instrument for a delicate job.

I'm not here to tell you to quit. I'm not here to shame you for using. I'm here to help you understand what this part is actually doing, so you can start to have a different relationship with it. Because when you fight this part, it fights back harder. When you shame it, it digs in deeper. When you try to kill it, it just finds another way to survive. The only path forward is to get curious about it. To sit with it. To ask it what it's really trying to accomplish.

What if the goal isn't to get rid of this part, but to help it find other ways to do its job? What if the substance is just the tool, and the real need is for relief, for safety, for a break from the constant hypervigilance? That changes everything. Suddenly you're not fighting a monster. You're trying to help a friend who's been working way too hard for way too long with inadequate resources.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). It helped me see that this part isn't separate from me. It's a piece of me that got frozen in time, still trying to cope with what happened.

What your nervous system is actually asking for

Stay with me here. Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. When it detects threat, it mobilizes. When it detects inescapable threat, it shuts down. And when neither of those works, it looks for anything that can change the state. That's where substances come in. They're a shortcut to a different nervous system state. A state that might feel like safety, even if it's chemically induced.

The problem is that the relief is temporary, and the cost is high. But here's what I want you to hear. Your nervous system isn't wrong for wanting relief. It's not wrong for using whatever tools it can find. The shame isn't yours to carry. It was placed on you by a culture that tells you that you should be able to handle everything on your own, without help, without crutches, without anything that makes it easier. That's a lie. A dangerous, cruel lie.

I remember sitting with my own part, the one that relied on alcohol to get through social situations. I asked it, "What are you trying to do for me?" And it said, "I'm trying to make you feel like you belong. I'm trying to quiet the part that's sure everyone is judging you. I'm trying to give you a few hours where you're not scanning the room for exits." Wild, right? It wasn't trying to destroy me. It was trying to give me something I'd never had. A sense of ease. A break from the constant vigilance.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work helped me start treating this part with compassion instead of contempt. It's a slow process, but it's the only one that actually works long term.

How to start a conversation with this part

You don't have to quit anything to start this work. You don't have to be sober to have compassion for yourself. You just have to be willing to get curious. Next time you reach for the substance, pause for one second. Just one. And ask yourself, "What am I feeling right now that's so unbearable?" Don't judge the answer. Don't try to fix it. Just notice it.

Maybe it's loneliness. Maybe it's a memory that's trying to surface. Maybe it's a body sensation that feels like it's going to tear you apart. Maybe it's nothing you can name, just a vague sense of wrongness that's been there as long as you can remember. Whatever it is, it's real. And this part has been carrying it for you.

The work isn't to eliminate this part. The work is to help it rest. To let it know that you're here now, that you're an adult with resources it didn't have when it was created. To show it that there are other ways to find relief. That you can handle the feelings together. That it doesn't have to do this alone anymore.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises that helped me map out which parts were doing what, including the ones I'd been fighting my whole life.

The real goal isn't abstinence. It's integration.

I'm not going to tell you that substances are fine and there's no problem. I'm not going to tell you that you should keep using. But I'm also not going to tell you that you have to quit right now or you're failing at recovery. That's toxic positivity dressed up as wellness, and it's killed more people than it's helped.

The real goal is to understand this part so deeply that you can start to meet its needs in other ways. Maybe that means finding a therapist who understands trauma and addiction. Maybe that means joining a support group where you don't have to pretend you have it together. Maybe that means learning to sit with your feelings for thirty seconds before reaching for the substance, and gradually building that window of tolerance.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helped me understand why I needed substances in the first place. Because I never learned that I could go to another human being for comfort. I only learned that I had to handle everything myself.

This part of you is not a mistake. It's not a stain on your soul. It's a survivor. It's doing exactly what it was designed to do. Keep you alive. Keep you functional. Keep you from feeling what felt impossible to feel. And it's been doing that job with zero support, zero appreciation, and a ton of shame heaped on it by a world that doesn't understand trauma.

So maybe today you don't have to change anything. Maybe today you just sit with this part and say, "I see you. I understand why you do what you do. And I'm going to start learning how to help you find another way. Not because you're bad. But because you deserve to rest."

Sit with that. Let it land. And know that this is where real change begins. Not with force. Not with shame. But with a kind of understanding that has been missing from your life for far too long.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - For understanding how trauma fragments the self
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - A book that many find validates their childhood experience
